narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family background. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 59.2% chance that you might fail, which is why your application was rejected—schools rely on this to ensure high success rates for their students. This prediction was driven primarily by your undergraduate GPA, which was a bit lower than what the model typically sees in successful applicants, pushing the odds toward failure more than any other factor. Your Year 1 school decile ranking also played a significant role in that direction, as it fell short of the stronger early performance that often correlates with bar success. On a brighter note, your Year 3 decile was above average, which helped pull the prediction back toward passing and showed your ability to improve over time. Your LSAT score, right around the typical level, added a small nudge toward failure, while your upper-middle family income had a modest influence in the same direction compared to peers. Being a full-time student, like most applicants, provided a slight positive boost toward passing. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having some standout areas like later academic gains and a competitive LSAT, but the early GPA and Year 1 performance stood out as weaker than average, tipping the scales. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or undergrad classes to boost your GPA, gaining more academic experience to improve those early deciles, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. You have the resilience to strengthen these areas and reapply stronger—keep pushing, because your story deserves to be told in the courtroom. We're rooting for you.
